author: @flightofthejellyfish
text:
I'm not sure what the Fresnel lens is meant to be doing. If it was focussing the light shouldn't it be making a bright spot in the middle of the solar panel. In which case you'd be better off with just the mirror spreading the heat over the entire panel.

Is the idea that the lens bends light from many angles onto the mirror/panel so that the mirror doesn't need to be solar tracking? In which case why would it give a higher output than just the well aligned mirror as Robert seemed to show in the video?

replies.author: @flightofthejellyfish
replies.text: Yeah okay answering myself, having now gone back and looked up "prism film" instead of imagining a Fresnel, it's the second one. The prism film bends the light to be more straight out from the mirror, giving a benefit without solar tracking. I guess the mirror was intentionally not aligned for the demonstration.

author: @TimeSurfer206

And as a proponent of Solar Steam Generation, I'm waiting for someone to make a LINEAR Fresnel Lens.

author: @davidpotter9462
text: I had somewhat of an idea how to do the same thing....I'll do it on the 3rd....I had already planned to get 3 more 100 watt panels so that lets me have 1000 watts out there...the amps will be around 30 coming in from the panels,  using an Epever mppt controller 40 amps...this will be just until August,  I will get two more batteries and that will make 14... I don't know if I will go to 16 later or not...I'm getting the 110 amp hours ones now, so the amps hours will be 635, at 24 volts, although it's almost always 25...I'm glad the battery buying stuff is about over,  hope I am getting enough batteries to run my Aims inverter/charger...I figure it's better just to have the system and not need the grid plug anymore...the sun is plenty hot here in Oklahoma without a reflection and it's fixing to storm so I may have to bring my panels inside...they're too much money to let them get hailed on so I'm making frames out of small square pipe around each two panels so I can grab them in...I really like it now that I've been off grid,  but it does cost at first and so it can't do as much for awhile,  if you build a bit  at a time like I'm doing it...but it's nice now...free energy...I can get 3 compact high output mono panels for around 200 bucks because of repeat customer discount so I'm going for it...they put out good amps...nobody tells you that it all runs on amps...lol

author: @vincentrobinette1507
text: You would also see a considerable gain in output, if the wire from the panel wasn't shadowing the panel. If you shade even one cell, the available current from the whole panel is only as much as the shaded cell.(s) reflecting light onto the panel increases current, but voltage decreases, as the cells heat up. Anything that will help cool the cells will increase the mean voltage of the panel, to multiply with the current, which is proportionate to the intensity of the light shining on the panel.

replies.author: @SinsBird
replies.text: I suggest you go grab a calculator now and calculate that 'considerable gain'.

replies.author: @vincentrobinette1507
replies.text: @SinsBird Since the cells in a panel are in a series circuit, the whole panel only produces as much current as its weakest cell. The gain by moving the shadowing cord goes far beyond the percentage of area it's shading.

author: @russellmoore1533
text:
This might work OK on a camping PV system, where you have small panels, and you can play around with angles etc, but on a roof top like mine you would need mirrors almost as large as the panels themselves and that would be huge in my case. Also most panels are not at a high angle such as they are in this video, but down around 25 degrees. Remember that if you don't get an even spread off light onto the panels, you can only get power from the panels equal to the spot on the panel receiving the least amount of light. As the sun moves through the sky the reflecting light will keep changing angles. All good in theory, not so good in practice.

My 5kW system was installed just over ten years ago, and I have water cooled them with an oscillating sprinkler on a 20 times a day timer with a 3min on 15min off cycle starting around 9:30am. The water runs from a rainwater tank, and recycles bak into the tank. I can get up to a 10% increase in power most of the time. I replaced my Fronious IG40 recently with a new SMA Sunny Boy inverter which is a little more efficient, and allowing for that, my panels have lost about 0.4% per year of power output, not bad I say.

author: @Thunderstormworld
text: Robert Murray-Smith I can increase the amount of power generated per PV solar cell. I will only give one secret away for you as you increase the amount of sunlight your PV solar cells start overheating and power drops. If you cool down the cells with either a aluminium heat sink or by means of water cooling the power increases. Also PV solar cells when overheating their life span decrease as these cells can become extremely hot in the sun and with cooling they last longer.

Oh, for sure it won’t account to much, but strangely, when solar panels have even a little shadow on them, output of the entire panel reduces a lot.

I would love to see perfect exposure panel output, and reflector added output.

The voltage increase isn’t necessarily showing WATTAGE output, which needs a load to calculate. So, running the panel through a controller into a large battery with wattage displayed, then add the reflectors and see if IMP wattage increases. It would be a far better test.

author: @KlevaOyibo
text:
A Fresnel Lens is just a lot of mini mirrors or mini lenses, depending on the light angle (angle of incidence), so this is perfectly feasible as you have created a lot of mini mirrors (although some are passing light, some are reflecting at an angle towards the panel). A well situated mirror would most likely perform better by directing all the light on its area at the solar panel rather than just a portion.
So, a focused mirror would outperform the Fresnel lens easily. Since mirrors can be expensive in large sizes, even a focused aluminium foil on board would work.
Would love to see you try the comparison, I have but am not a sci-guy or with equipment for Youtube, but I love photovoltaic and all solar experiments, and have had to learn a lot to become off-grid at home.
Thank you for your channel, I really wish I could play :D

author: @robertjenkins5440
text: I am not an electronic's engineer and may sound like a moron for asking...i have asked another person before...if you can't improve the solar efficiency much...then can you put a voltage doubler of some type on the output side to get more power...i'm sorry if i sound stupid....but you don't learn if you don't ask...i tinker around with scrapping out ton's of the thing's and like to reuse good part's....can someone give me a real answer on this question? Please

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate a voltage doubler will double the voltage for sure - but it does that at the expense of amps so no more power is gained. As the doubler circuit takes power to run you actually get less power from the system but you do get double the voltage and sometimes that is worth the reduction in power

replies.author: @SinsBird
replies.text: No. Since power P = voltage V * current I, if you used a boost converter to double the voltage you would still get the same power (actually a bit less because there are power losses dissipated as heat in the boost converter itself) but half of the current.

replies.author: @robertjenkins5440
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering thank you for answering my question Robert...is there any way to increase the amperage? I love your experiment's...very interesting stuff...

replies.author: @robertjenkins5440
replies.text: @SinsBird thank's for the info...i am interested in this stuff but don't know much about how it all work's...👍

replies.author: @SinsBird
replies.text: @robertjenkins5440 To increase the amperage, you would need a buck converter, which would increase the amperage while lowering the voltage.

author: @sailingoctopus1
text:
Buildings oriented East-West could be designed to have south/north-facing cat slide roofs (depending on hemisphere), with two pitches, one pitch covered with solar panels angled to optimise the solar energy and a second pitch covered with a mirrored finish roofing material overlayed with lenticular film (Fresnel lens) angled to focus the reflected light on the pitch covered with solar panels. This would increase the output of the solar panels at a significantly lower overall roofing cost.

Interestingly, where we are situated we are south-facing and have the sea to the South, with the sea acting a little bit like a lenticular lens, so we benefit from reflected light, which makes our winters noticeably lighter than would otherwise be the case.

author: @sofa-lofa4241
text:
I think the gain is not quite as much as one would think, this is because of the fact that as you heat a panel it lowers in voltage (0.45% per °C) and efficiency, as the amps increase as the voltage decreases,
The figures in the Hyperstealth Biotech video are measured at 10am in one test and on December 6th for another (possibly the same day?) this is not a test of a hot panel, I think if the ambient temperature is low enough then just mirrors would work about as well, and if it were tested in full midday sunlight in July/August then panel heating issues would mean that the net gain would be very low, as the reflected light could make panel temperature skyrocket to 50 - 70°C

Panels are tested by manufacturers at 25°C, this means that the manufacturers rating is already slightly off in hot, sunny weather as the panel is probably around 40 - 50°C in these conditions, in severe cases this could mean a loss of around 15 - 20%

I think the increase from this system would be useful in the Winter but cause issues/damage in Summer as the extra heat would shorten the life of the panel

author: @SinsBird
text:
So I did some tests:
Cooled the panel by pouring water on it:
1st try: went from 0.5A to 0.63A +26% (big improvement, might be an outlier).
2nd try: went from 0.55A to 0.6A +9%.
Directed light with a mirror (kinda dirty):
Went from 0.6A to 0.72A +20%.
Mirror (kinda dirty) + prison film
Went from 0.58A to 0.81A 40%.
Prison film by itself performed pretty poorly.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate - seems like it is prism film and mirror with cooling is the way forward?

replies.author: @SinsBird
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yup.

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
Hi SinsBird, I would be interested if you could test again for total output power in watts, also could you setup the panel and take readings and then take readings after the panel has got to full temperature in midday sun, and note the panel temperature, 
This would take into account the fact that as the current increases the voltage is decreasing due to the temperature (0.45% per 1°C increase)
I think this might give a better understanding of the full picture

replies.author: @SinsBird
replies.text: @sofa-lofa4241 I would like to, but I don't have my infrared thermometer with me at the moment.

replies.author: @bazofiascbr
replies.text:
@SinsBird Do you use an MPPT controller? As far as I know temperature decrease of solar cells will increase _tension_output_, an MPPT controller will look for the sweet-spot of the tension/current ratio. Could you try compare voltages too, with the different arrangements? This could demonstrate even higher output increases. Or try as @Sofa-Lofa suggested, measure actual power output?
Good job doing that testing!

replies.author: @bobleponge1301
replies.text: if the panel is impragnated in resin (like the new ones) and are waterproof. Than you can drop it in an aquarium like or have water running over the surface. Temperature drops and efficiency goes up. Another way around woud be to concentrate at on point, install a prism to remove infrareds(and UV) and return the ''clean'' light to the solar panel. So you will have just light but no heat.

replies.author: @bobleponge1301
replies.text: @lexiecrewther7038 Light is NOT ONLY heat. IR is heat, the rest of the visible spectrum is not heat. Heat drops output of solar panels. We are talking CONCENTRATING large amount of light on the solar panels. Would you prefer to loose a little on the output or fry your panel?

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I've had a keen eye on Guy Cramer for years now. It started with his "passive negative-ion generator" patent (awesome work.) When he announced Hyperstealth years ago, I had no idea that it would take almost 10 years for it to be patented. I'm glad you looked at his work and thank you for all your awesome content!!

author: @rogerstarkey5390
text:
Robert, next time you see a bus, look at the rear window.
(Fresnel lens!)
You can focus and deflect light passing *through* the lens rather than reflecting it.
So lenses raised above the panel in the same plane, set atound the perimeter (wide, raised picture frame?) should be able to "bend" light that was going to strike outside the edge onto the panel.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I know mate but that only really works with spot lenses - it doesn't work using linear lenses which is the type found in lcd screens - check this out https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x_UKVEBxYFM

replies.author: @rogerstarkey5390
